title: Not Funny Anymore: LLM Judges Confuse Literal Similarity for Humor in Translated Jokes
research_questions: Can large language models reliably evaluate whether humor is preserved in cross-lingual joke translation based on cross-lingual reasoning?
Are poor LM evaluator results in humor translation evaluation driven by over-attending to literalness as a signal for quality?
tasks: humor preservation evaluation in joke translation

datasets_used.name: Kaggle short-jokes dataset
datasets_used.size: 162 jokes sampled for Mandarin; 76 jokes for Hindi

datasets_used.name: English-Chinese and English-Hindi humor preservation benchmarking dataset
datasets_used.size: 162 English-to-Chinese joke-translation pairs and 76 English-to-Hindi joke-translation pairs

methods_proposed.name: Reference-free humor translation benchmarking framework
methods_proposed.summary: The paper evaluates LLM-as-a-Judge systems on joke-translation pairs using only the original joke and its translation, asking models to rate humor preservation on a 1–5 Likert scale and comparing the resulting ratings against human annotations.

methods_proposed.name: Token-level semantic alignment / literalness metric
methods_proposed.summary: The paper introduces a multilingual token-level semantic diagnostic using xlm-r-100langs-bert-base-nli-stsb-mean-tokens embeddings, Procrustes alignment, cosine similarity, and Hungarian matching to compute a per-joke literalness score. Higher scores are interpreted as translations that more closely follow the source wording and structure.

evaluation_methods.detail: Four bilingual native speakers annotated the Mandarin translations and three annotators annotated the Hindi translations; each rated all jokes on a five-point Likert scale based on semantic preservation of humor. The paper reports Krippendorff’s α of 0.776 for Mandarin and “0.776 (change number)” for Hindi.

Seven LLMs rated each source-translation joke pair under three prompting strategies: vanilla, chain-of-thought, and self-consistency. Prompts instructed models to output a single integer score from 1 to 5 prioritizing humor preservation over surface lexical similarity.

Model ratings were compared to human ratings using strict agreement, ±1 accuracy, MAE, Spearman correlation, Pearson correlation, and binary classification metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1.

A token-level semantic alignment score was computed as a literalness/wordiness diagnostic, using multilingual embeddings, Procrustes alignment, Hungarian token matching, and mean token-level cosine similarity.

headline_results.finding: The highest strict agreement in Table 1 was achieved by Gemini with self-consistency prompting.
Average strict accuracy across prompting strategies was highest for Gemini and lowest for OpenAI.
The paper states that Spearman and Pearson correlations are low across models and prompts.
The best ±1 accuracy and lowest MAE in Table 3 were achieved by Mistral with vanilla prompting.
The highest binary classification accuracy in Table 2 was achieved by Mistral with vanilla prompting.
Human annotator agreement for the Mandarin annotations was reported as Krippendorff’s α of 0.776.
headline_results.dataset: English-Chinese and English-Hindi humor preservation benchmarking dataset

key_findings: Across 7 models and 3 prompting strategies, strict agreement with human 1–5 humor-preservation ratings was often near or below the 20% random baseline.
The best strict agreement reported was only 23.5 for Gemini with self-consistency prompting, while model average strict accuracies ranged from 9.1 to 15.8.
Spearman and Pearson correlations were low, described as mostly between −0.15 and 0.27, indicating that LLM judges poorly tracked human rank order of funniness.
The token-level literalness analysis showed that LLMs tend to give higher ratings to more literal translations, even when humans judge humor to be lost.
The error analysis argues that LLM judges fail especially on jokes whose humor depends on idioms, wordplay, or linguistic ambiguity, while alignment is better for content-based humor.
Chain-of-thought and self-consistency prompting did not eliminate the misalignment between LLM and human humor judgments.
limitations: The paper states that the dataset is limited to jokes translated from English to Chinese only and that results cannot be assumed to generalize across languages with different humor traditions.
The dataset relies on synthetic translations generated by ChatGPT rather than human-translated jokes, which may bias it toward machine-friendly structures.
The paper notes possible self-preference because GPT-4.1 was used as a rater for translations done by gpt4o-mini.
The dataset is relatively small, with 162 pairs, which can reduce statistical significance.
The jokes are mostly short-form one-liners and exclude longer forms of humor such as narratives, irony, and satire.
The paper says heavy reliance on wordplay and puns makes the findings less applicable to humor forms relying on situational context.
Findings are restricted to current SoTA LLMs including GPT-4, Claude, and Gemini, and future models may behave differently.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper states that all annotators were volunteering friends of the authors; no additional safety or ethics discussion is provided.

artifacts.other: The paper states that the dataset will be publicly released in Hugging Face upon acceptance.

human_samples.population: bilingual native speakers serving as Mandarin annotators; all annotators were volunteering friends of the authors
human_samples.observations: Each annotator rated all 162 English-to-Mandarin joke-translation pairs on a five-point Likert scale for semantic preservation of humor; Krippendorff’s alpha reported as 0.776.
human_samples.detail: The paper does not report age, gender, location, compensation, training, or whether annotators were blinded to the translation source.

human_samples.population: Hindi annotators; all annotators were volunteering friends of the authors
human_samples.observations: The Hindi process used 76 joke-translation pairs and 3 annotators; the paper says the same process was repeated and reports Krippendorff’s alpha as “0.776 (change number).”
human_samples.detail: The Hindi agreement value appears to include an unresolved placeholder. The paper does not separately describe Hindi annotator demographics, location, training, compensation, or blinding.

human_samples.population: contributors/authors represented in the Kaggle short-jokes dataset, not described in the paper
human_samples.observations: The authors sampled 162 jokes from Kaggle for Mandarin translation and 76 jokes for Hindi translation; these are item counts, not contributor counts.
human_samples.detail: No information is given about the people who originally wrote or contributed the Kaggle jokes.

judges.name: bilingual human annotators
judges.detail: Human annotators supplied the reference 1–5 humor-preservation ratings against which LLM-generated ratings were compared. The paper describes them as bilingual native speakers/friends, not professional evaluators.

judges.name: Claude, GPT-4o/OpenAI, Gemini, Qwen-3, DeepSeek-3, Llama-4, and Mistral
judges.detail: Seven LLMs rated each source-translation joke pair under vanilla, chain-of-thought, and self-consistency prompting. Prompts asked for a single integer 1–5 score prioritizing humor preservation over surface lexical similarity.

judges.name: agreement/correlation/classification metrics
judges.detail: Strict agreement, ±1 accuracy, MAE, Spearman correlation, Pearson correlation, and binary classification metrics were computed from LLM ratings versus human ratings.

judges.name: token-level semantic alignment / literalness score
judges.detail: The paper computes a literalness/wordiness diagnostic using xlm-r-100langs-bert-base-nli-stsb-mean-tokens embeddings, Procrustes alignment, cosine similarity, Hungarian matching, and mean token-level cosine similarity.

generation_selection.scope: Machine-translation dataset construction: English jokes translated to Mandarin and Hindi
generation_selection.n: One final translation per sampled joke is reported: 162 Mandarin pairs and 76 Hindi pairs; no candidate pool size is reported.
generation_selection.detail: Translations were generated using GPT-4o-mini/ChatGPT-generated translation. The paper does not describe best-of-N generation, manual selection, or filtering among multiple candidate translations.

generation_selection.scope: LLM judge ratings under vanilla and chain-of-thought prompts
generation_selection.n: One final integer rating per model-prompt-joke pair
generation_selection.detail: The paper says each model was queried with each prompting strategy and instructed to output a single integer score. Repeated samples or majority voting are not reported.

generation_selection.scope: LLM judge ratings under self-consistency prompt
generation_selection.detail: The prompt asks the model to “internally compare multiple possible ratings and choose the most consistent score,” but the paper does not report external multiple samples, number of internal ratings, or a best-of-N procedure.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o-mini
model_provenance.detail: Used to translate sampled English jokes into Mandarin; limitations also describe the translations as ChatGPT-generated and “done by gpt4o-mini.” No snapshot, API date, prompt, or decoding settings are reported.

model_provenance.name: Claude Sonnet
model_provenance.detail: Listed in the Model details section as one of the judge models.

model_provenance.name: OpenAI judge model
model_provenance.detail: Experimental setup/model details list gpt-4o, while the Limitations section says “GPT-4.1 as a rater,” creating an unresolved inconsistency.

model_provenance.name: Gemini
model_provenance.detail: Listed in the Model details section; no API snapshot or inference date is given.

model_provenance.name: Qwen3
model_provenance.detail: Listed in the Model details section as a judge model.

model_provenance.name: Llama 4
model_provenance.detail: Listed in the Model details section as a judge model.

model_provenance.name: Mistral
model_provenance.detail: Listed in the Model details section as a judge model.

model_provenance.name: DeepSeek
model_provenance.detail: Listed in the Model details section as a judge model.

model_provenance.name: xlm-r-100langs-bert-base-nli-stsb-mean-tokens
model_provenance.detail: Used to encode tokens for the semantic alignment/literalness metric; no model source, runtime, or version hash is reported.

inference_budgets.scope: GPT-4o-mini translation generation for dataset construction
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: One final translation per source joke is present in the dataset; the number of generated candidates is not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: The paper does not report translation prompt, batching, temperature, top-p, max tokens, retries, cost, or inference date.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Each of 7 models is queried with 3 prompting strategies for each joke-translation pair, i.e. 21 model-prompt judgments per pair as described.
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: One final integer rating per model/prompt is reported; no external multi-sample self-consistency count is reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Prompts are provided, but decoding parameters, max tokens, API/provider routes, inference dates, retries, and total cost are not reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Each source joke and translation are tokenized/encoded with the multilingual embedding model; exact batching or call counts are not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: The paper reports the algorithmic steps but not runtime, hardware, embedding-model version hash, or implementation details for estimating the Procrustes alignment matrix.

human_baselines.description: Human annotators rated the same source-translation joke pairs on a 1–5 Likert scale, and LLM ratings were compared against the human annotation vector.
human_baselines.detail: The paper states that LLMs were tasked with the same evaluation and restricted to the original joke and translation as input. However, the exact aggregation of multiple human ratings into h_i is not consistently specified.

human_baselines.description: No human-translated joke outputs are used as a matched baseline for the GPT-4o-mini synthetic translations.
human_baselines.detail: The paper’s translation pairs are synthetic/ChatGPT-generated; it evaluates judges, not competing human versus machine translators.

mitigations: The translation pairs and human ratings appear newly created for this work rather than taken from an existing labeled benchmark.
The paper says the dataset will be publicly released upon acceptance, implying the full labeled benchmark was not already public at paper time.
contamination_risk.basis: The English source jokes were sampled from the public Kaggle short-jokes dataset, so evaluated LLMs may have encountered the joke texts or close variants in pretraining; no memorization or decontamination test is reported. Risk is reduced because the specific GPT-4o-mini translations and human humor-preservation labels appear newly created, but the paper does not report dataset creation dates relative to model training or inference. The paper discusses synthetic-translation/self-preference bias, but not contamination.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: Headline claims that LLM judges struggle, that strict agreement is near or below the 20% random baseline, and that LLM evaluator errors are driven by literalness all depend on ratings produced by the evaluated LLM judges.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Human ratings from 4 Mandarin annotators and 3 Hindi annotators are used as the comparison target; Mandarin Krippendorff’s alpha is reported as 0.776, while Hindi alpha is reported with an unresolved “change number” placeholder.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: LLM judging is the object of study rather than an unvalidated replacement for all evaluation. Still, the main empirical results are determined by LLM-generated ratings compared with human annotations and by an automatic literalness metric.

reporting_gaps: Hindi coverage is inconsistent: the abstract and Dataset section report 76 English-to-Hindi pairs, but the Limitations section says the dataset is limited to English-to-Chinese only and refers to 162 pairs.
Results tables do not clearly state whether metrics are computed on Mandarin only, Hindi only, both languages combined, or some subset.
The Hindi Krippendorff’s alpha is printed as “0.776 (change number),” suggesting an unresolved placeholder.

The aggregation of multiple human ratings into the human annotation vector h_i is unclear; the paper variously refers to a human annotation vector, human rater mode, average human-assigned scores, and example average human ratings.
Human annotator demographics, geography, training, compensation, and blinding are not reported beyond bilingual/native-speaker status and being volunteering friends of the authors.
The sampling procedure for selecting jokes from the Kaggle short-jokes dataset is not described.
GPT-4o-mini translation-generation prompt, sampling parameters, candidate counts, filtering/selection, inference date, and access route are not reported.
LLM judge access route, inference date, decoding parameters, max-token limits, retries, and costs are not reported.
OpenAI judge provenance is inconsistent: model details list gpt-4o, while the Limitations section mentions GPT-4.1 as a rater.
The operational details of self-consistency are underspecified; no number of samples, votes, or repeated calls is reported.
The binary classification threshold or mapping from 1–5 ratings to binary labels is not defined.
Details of the Procrustes alignment estimation for the literalness metric are underspecified, including what data are used to fit W and whether the method is applied identically to Hindi.
No contamination or memorization check is reported for the public Kaggle source jokes.
No confidence intervals, significance tests, or uncertainty estimates are reported for the main accuracy/correlation metrics.
